Curriculum → Data storage and indexing → The NoSQL landscape

Lesson 4 of 5

The NoSQL landscape

NoSQL systems trade some relational flexibility for scale, availability patterns, schema freedom, or specialized query models.

25-35 min Data storage and indexing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Interview answers improve when you can say not only that NoSQL is scalable, but what kind and at what semantic cost.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Key-value and document stores Great for profiles, sessions, feature flags, and metadata2. Wide-column and graph stores Wide-column systems fit high-write event or telemetry workloads3. Use polyglot persistence thoughtfully Pick the primary store per domain invariant

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Interview answers improve when you can say not only that NoSQL is scalable, but what kind and at what semantic cost. 2. Key-value and document stores Great for profiles, sessions, feature flags, and metadata3. Wide-column and graph stores Wide-column systems fit high-write event or telemetry workloads4. Use polyglot persistence thoughtfully Pick the primary store per domain invariant
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Interview answers improve when you can say not only that NoSQL is scalable, but what kind and at what semantic cost.

What to say first

  • Explain which access pattern motivates the chosen store.
  • Name the semantic trade-off, not only the scaling benefit.
  • Use polyglot persistence intentionally.
2

Study section

Key-value and document stores

Key-value stores excel at simple lookups and caching, while document stores fit evolving, nested records with access patterns centered on a document boundary.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Great for profiles, sessions, feature flags, and metadata
  • Watch document growth and secondary index cost
  • Denormalization is usually expected rather than exceptional
3

Study section

Wide-column and graph stores

Wide-column stores optimize very large sparse datasets and partition-friendly access patterns; graph databases help when traversals are the product itself.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Wide-column systems fit high-write event or telemetry workloads
  • Graph databases fit social edges, recommendations, or relationship queries
  • Operational maturity and query ergonomics vary widely
4

Study section

Use polyglot persistence thoughtfully

One product can legitimately use relational storage for orders, object storage for media, search indexes for retrieval, and Redis for ephemeral state.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Pick the primary store per domain invariant
  • Avoid duplicating the same truth without a clear pipeline
  • Plan synchronization and ownership carefully

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

Wide-column stores favor partition-friendly access patterns over ad hoc relational flexibility.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Explain which access pattern motivates the chosen store.
  • Name the semantic trade-off, not only the scaling benefit.
  • Use polyglot persistence intentionally.
  • Keep one source of truth per domain whenever possible.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Calling every distributed store a NoSQL database without specifying the model.
  • Moving to NoSQL to avoid schema work rather than because the workload benefits.
  • Ignoring index, consistency, and repair costs.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • When would a document store beat a relational schema?
  • What workloads fit wide-column systems?
  • How would you keep a search index synchronized with the primary store?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Choosing the right storage mix Data storage and indexingCase study: query-result cache Case studies and interview drills

Interactive topic lab

NoSQL pattern explorer

A strong NoSQL answer names the exact model, the access pattern it helps, and the semantic cost you accept in return. “NoSQL because scale” is too shallow for interviews.

Different NoSQL families optimize different shapes of work.Partition keys matter more than brand names.Polyglot persistence only works when ownership boundaries stay clear.

How to explain it

High-signal talking points

  • Different NoSQL families optimize different shapes of work.
  • Partition keys matter more than brand names.
  • Polyglot persistence only works when ownership boundaries stay clear.

Interactive diagram

Polyglot persistence done intentionally

Rendering diagram…

Give each storage engine a narrow, explainable job instead of letting multiple stores compete for the same truth.

Worked example

Document storage fits product records that evolve by feature area

A profile service stores bios, privacy settings, notification preferences, and feature flags that change at different speeds.

Recommended choice

A document store works well when the application usually reads and writes one profile at a time.

Why it fits

  • Nested user settings map naturally to a document boundary.
  • Feature teams can evolve the payload without coordinating every change through multi-table migrations.
  • The read path stays simple because the product almost always needs the whole profile.

If you chose differently

A relational schema is still fine if joins and reporting matter more than payload flexibility, but forcing every variant into rigid tables can slow iteration.

Interview-ready takeaway

The best explanation is not “documents are modern,” but “the access pattern is profile-by-id with evolving nested fields.”

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: The NoSQL landscape

Use the simulator to see when primary, replica, shard, or queue pressure should change your storage story. Apply it to the nosql landscape so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "The NoSQL landscape" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around key-value and document stores and watch how it interacts with wide-column and graph stores.Interview answers improve when you can say not only that NoSQL is scalable, but what kind and at what semantic cost.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • read p95
  • replica utilization
  • shard pressure
  • cache hit ratio

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Exercise workspace

Multi-file topology and override editor

Use file tabs, code completion, inline previews, and diagnostics to tune the topology and scripted overrides without leaving the browser.

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 1: edge · 100000 rps · 4 ms
  • Line 2: service · 38000 rps · 10 ms
  • Line 3: cache · 120000 rps · 3 ms
  • Line 4: database · 8500 rps · 20 ms
  • Line 5: database · 17000 rps · 16 ms
  • Line 6: database · 21000 rps · 18 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Explain which access pattern motivates the chosen store.
  • Name the semantic trade-off, not only the scaling benefit.
  • Use polyglot persistence intentionally.

Interactive diagram

Compiled simulation topology

Rendering diagram…

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from Key-value and document stores and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like Explain which access pattern motivates the chosen store. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from Wide-column and graph stores.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

interview

Explain the concept

Not saved yet

Teach "The NoSQL landscape" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal for this step

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Use these guardrails

  • When would a document store beat a relational schema?
  • Key-value and document stores
  • Wide-column and graph stores
  • Explain which access pattern motivates the chosen store.

Suggested structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
